content:
# 2 John — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** 2 John (Greek: Iōannou B) | **Chapters:** 1

## At a Glance
- **Author:** "The elder" (ho presbyteros, v. 1) — not named. Traditionally identified with the apostle John. Papias (in Eusebius, Church History 3.39.4) distinguishes between "John the apostle" and "John the elder" — whether these are the same person or two different figures is debated.
- **Date:** c. 90–110 CE, contemporary with 1 John.
- **Audience:** "The elect lady and her children" (v. 1) — either a specific woman or (more likely) a personification of a local church.
- **Genre:** Letter — the shortest book in the NT by word count (in Greek). A genuine letter with greeting, body, and closing.
- **Timeline:** c. 90–110 CE.
- **In One Sentence:** 2 John is a brief letter from "the elder" warning a church against receiving traveling teachers who deny that Jesus Christ came in the flesh — the same docetic threat addressed in 1 John.

## The World Behind the Text
Early Christian communities relied on traveling teachers and missionaries for instruction (Didache 11–13, c. 100 CE, addresses this practice and its abuses). 2 John addresses the practical question: should communities offer hospitality to teachers whose doctrine the elder considers false?

## Why This Book Was Written
The letter's core message: "Many deceivers, who do not acknowledge Jesus Christ as coming in the flesh, have gone out into the world. Any such person is the deceiver and the antichrist" (v. 7). The instruction: "If anyone comes to you and does not bring this teaching, do not take them into your house or welcome them" (v. 10). In a world without hotels, refusing hospitality effectively silenced traveling teachers.

## How to Read This Book
**vv. 1–3:** Greeting. **vv. 4–6:** Walking in truth and love. **vv. 7–11:** Warning against deceivers. **vv. 12–13:** Closing — "I have much to write to you, but I do not want to use paper and ink. Instead, I hope to visit you and talk with you face to face" (v. 12). The mention of "paper and ink" (chartēs kai melanos) references the physical writing materials.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
2 John v. 7 uses the same language as 1 John 4:2–3 — the test of confessing Jesus "in the flesh." The "antichrist" terminology (v. 7) is shared only with 1 John.

The "elect lady" (eklektē kyria) greeting is mirrored in the closing: "The children of your elect sister greet you" (v. 13) — one church sending greetings to another, using familial/feminine personification.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **This is a real letter** — unlike 1 John, which lacks letter conventions, 2 John has a greeting, body, and closing. It is the shortest book in the NT.
- **"Do not take them into your house" (v. 10)** is a practical instruction with significant social consequences — in the ancient world, refusing hospitality was a public act of repudiation.
- **"The elder" as a self-designation** suggests a figure of authority — whether by age, office, or apostolic status. The title connects to Papias's mention of "the elder John."
- **P — no papyrus of 2 John survives before the 4th century.** The earliest complete witness is Codex Sinaiticus (though P⁰ from the 4th century preserves fragments). 2 John's brevity may account for limited manuscript attestation.

## Timeline
- **c. 90–110 CE:** Composition
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus — earliest complete manuscript

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
